---
title: "Bayesian Additive Regression Trees (BART)"
author:
  - "Jake Fortuna, Tyler Johnson, Ryan Pini,"
  - "Ashley Powell, Samika Reddy, and Kathleen Reece"
date: '`r Sys.Date()`'
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    self_contained: true
    css: ["default", "xaringan-themer.css", "custom.css"]
    nature:
      slideNumberFormat: "%current%"
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      ratio: 16:9
      countIncrementalSlides: true
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
options(htmltools.dir.version = FALSE)
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  fig.width=8, fig.height=2.5, fig.retina=3,
  out.width = "100%",
  cache = FALSE,
  echo = TRUE,
  comment = NA, # Kathleen added this to remove the hashtags
  message = FALSE, 
  warning = FALSE,
  hiline = TRUE
)
```

```{r xaringan-themer, include=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(xaringanthemer)
style_duo_accent(
  primary_color = "#1381B0",
  secondary_color = "#FF961C",
  inverse_header_color = "#FFFFFF"
)
library(ggplot2)
theme_set(theme_bw())
```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:


---
## BART vs LOESS: Regression Example
We use the `mcycle` dataset to predict **acceleration** using **time**. We are going to do this using BART as a tree-based regression method versus a LOESS regression which as discussed  in our class focuses on smoothing.

How our models are fit.
- Both BART and LOESS are fit with a 70% split for training data and 30% testing.
- LOESS is fit using different span values with span chosen using test MSE
- BART is fit using posterior draws from many trees and averaging
```{r, echo=FALSE, results='hide'}
library(MASS)
library(dbarts)
data(mcycle)
```
```{r}
set.seed(123)
x <- mcycle$times
y <- mcycle$accel
n <- length(y)
# Randomly choose 70% of the observations for training
train_idx <- sample(1:n, size = floor(0.7 * n))
x_train <- x[train_idx]
y_train <- y[train_idx]
x_test <- x[-train_idx]
y_test <- y[-train_idx]
# matrix form predictors for dbarts 
x_train_bart <- as.matrix(x_train)
x_test_bart  <- as.matrix(x_test)
```
```{r, echo=TRUE, results='hide'}
spans <- c(0.25, 0.35, 0.50, 0.65, 0.75)

# Vectors to store LOESS train/test errors
loess_train_mse <- numeric(length(spans))
loess_test_mse  <- numeric(length(spans))
# Fit one LOESS model for each span value
for (i in 1:length(spans)) {
  # Fit LOESS model using training data
  loess_fit <- loess(y_train ~ x_train,span = spans[i],degree = 1
  )
    
  # Predict acceleration for training data
  loess_train_pred <- predict(
    loess_fit,
    newdata = data.frame(x_train = x_train)
  )
  
  # Predict acceleration for testing data
  loess_test_pred <- predict(
    loess_fit,
    newdata = data.frame(x_train = x_test)
  )
  
  # Compute training MSE for this span
  loess_train_mse[i] <- mean(
    (y_train - loess_train_pred)^2,
    na.rm = TRUE
  )
  
  # Compute testing MSE for this span
  loess_test_mse[i] <- mean(
    (y_test - loess_test_pred)^2,
    na.rm = TRUE
  )
}


# Find the LOESS span with the lowest test error
best_loess_index <- which.min(loess_test_mse)

# Save the best LOESS train and test error
loess_best_train <- loess_train_mse[best_loess_index]
loess_best_test  <- loess_test_mse[best_loess_index]

```
```{r}
# Fit BART using dbarts
# ntree  = number of trees
# ndpost = number of posterior draws saved
# nskip  = number of burn-in draws skipped
bart_model <- bart( x.train = x_train_bart, y.train = y_train, x.test  = x_test_bart,ntree = 100, ndpost = 500, nskip = 200
)

```

---
### Calculate Prediction Accuracies
```{r, echo=TRUE, results='hide'}
# Extract posterior predictions for train and test sets
bart_train_draws <- bart_model$yhat.train
bart_test_draws  <- bart_model$yhat.test
# Make sure rows are posterior draws and columns are observations
if (ncol(bart_train_draws) != length(y_train)) {
  bart_train_draws <- t(bart_train_draws)
}
if (ncol(bart_test_draws) != length(y_test)) {
  bart_test_draws <- t(bart_test_draws)
}
# Number of posterior draws
n_draws <- nrow(bart_train_draws)
# Vectors to store dbarts train/test MSE for each posterior draw
bart_train_mse <- numeric(n_draws)
bart_test_mse  <- numeric(n_draws)
# Compute MSE for each posterior draw
for (i in 1:n_draws) {
  # Training MSE for posterior draw i
  bart_train_mse[i] <- mean((y_train - bart_train_draws[i, ])^2)
  # Testing MSE for posterior draw i
  bart_test_mse[i] <- mean((y_test - bart_test_draws[i, ])^2)
}
```
---

### Compare the accuracies

```{r, echo=TRUE, results='hide'}
# Smooth the dbarts errors using cumulative averages
bart_train_mse_smooth <- cumsum(bart_train_mse)/seq_along(bart_train_mse)
bart_test_mse_smooth  <- cumsum(bart_test_mse) / seq_along(bart_test_mse)
# Average dbarts train and test error across posterior draws
bart_final_train <- mean(bart_train_mse)
bart_final_test  <- mean(bart_test_mse)
```
BART error is shown as the average test MSE across posterior draws, while LOESS is shown as a constant line using its best span. This allows us to compare BART’s convergence to LOESS’s best performance.

```{r, eval=T, echo=F, fig.width=4, fig.height=3.5,  out.width="30%"}

# This plot shows how LOESS error changes as span changes
plot(spans, loess_train_mse,
     type = "b",
     pch = 19,
     col = "blue",
     lwd = 2,
     ylim = range(c(loess_train_mse, loess_test_mse), na.rm = TRUE),
     xlab = "LOESS Span",
     ylab = "Mean Squared Error",
     main = "LOESS Train and Test Error")

lines(spans, loess_test_mse,
      type = "b",
      pch = 19,
      col = "red",
      lty = 2,
      lwd = 2)

legend("topright",
       legend = c("LOESS Train MSE", "LOESS Test MSE"),
       col = c("blue", "red"),
       lty = c(1, 2),
       pch = c(19, 19),
       lwd = 2,
       cex = 0.8,  
      bty = "n" )



# This plot shows how dbarts error changes across posterior draws
plot(bart_train_mse_smooth,
     type = "l",
     col = "darkgreen",
     lwd = 2,
     ylim = range(c(bart_train_mse_smooth, bart_test_mse_smooth)),
     xlab = "Posterior Draw",
     ylab = "Mean Squared Error",
     main = "BART Train and Test Error")

lines(bart_test_mse_smooth,
      col = "orange",
      lty = 2,
      lwd = 2)

legend("topright",
       legend = c("BART Train MSE", "BART Test MSE"),
       col = c("darkgreen", "orange"),
       lty = c(1, 2),
       lwd = 2,
       cex = 0.8,  
      bty = "n" )


# LOESS gives one best train/test error value after choosing the best span.
# To compare it with dbarts across posterior draws, repeat the LOESS error
# values as horizontal reference lines.
loess_train_line <- rep(loess_best_train, n_draws)
loess_test_line  <- rep(loess_best_test, n_draws)

# Plot dbarts train error first
plot(bart_train_mse_smooth,
     type = "l",
     col = "darkgreen",
     lwd = 2,
     ylim = range(c(bart_train_mse_smooth,
                    bart_test_mse_smooth,
                    loess_train_line,
                    loess_test_line)),
     xlab = "Posterior Draw",
     ylab = "Mean Squared Error",
     main = "LOESS vs BART: Train and Test Error")

# Add dbarts test error
lines(bart_test_mse_smooth,
      col = "orange",
      lwd = 2,
      lty = 2)

# Add LOESS train error as a horizontal line
lines(loess_train_line,
      col = "blue",
      lwd = 2)

# Add LOESS test error as a horizontal dashed line
lines(loess_test_line,
      col = "red",
      lwd = 2,
      lty = 2)

legend("topright",
       legend = c("BART Train", "BART Test", "LOESS Train best span", "LOESS Test best span"),
       col = c("darkgreen", "orange", "blue", "red"),
       lty = c(1, 2, 1, 2),
       lwd = 2,
       cex = 0.8,  
      bty = "n" )


```
---
## Regression: Quantitative Results
```{r, eval=TRUE, echo=FALSE, results='asis', fig.width=4, fig.height=3,  out.width="80%"}
cat("<div style='margin-top: -45px; margin-bottom: -60px;'>")


cat('
<div style="display: flex; gap: 1bart_train_mse[i] <- mean((y_train - bart_train_draws[i, ])^2)
0px;">
<div style="width: 50%;">
')

error_values <- c(
  loess_best_train,
  loess_best_test,
  bart_final_train,
  bart_final_test
)

bar_colors <- c("blue", "red", "darkgreen", "orange")

barplot(error_values,
        names.arg = c("LOESS\nTrain", "LOESS\nTest",
                      "dbarts\nTrain", "dbarts\nTest"),
        col = bar_colors,
        ylab = "MSE",
        main = "Error Comparison",
        ylim = c(0, max(error_values) * 1.25),
        cex.names = 0.75,
        cex.main = 0.9,
        cex.lab = 0.8,
        cex.axis = 0.75)

legend("topright",
       legend = c("LOESS Train", "LOESS Test",
                  "dbarts Train", "dbarts Test"),
       fill = bar_colors,
       cex = 0.6,
       bty = "n")

cat('
</div>
<div style="width: 45%; font-size: 11px; display: flex; justify-content: center; align-items: center;">
')

comparison_df <- data.frame(
  Metric = c("Tuning / Draws", "Training MSE", "Testing MSE"),
  LOESS = c(
    paste("Best span =", spans[best_loess_index]),
    round(loess_best_train, 4),
    round(loess_best_test, 4)
  ),
  dbarts = c(
    paste("Posterior draws =", n_draws),
    round(bart_final_train, 4),
    round(bart_final_test, 4)
  )
)

print(
  knitr::kable(
    comparison_df,
    caption = "LOESS vs BART Regression Error Comparison",
    align = "c"
  )
)

cat('
</div>
</div>
')

cat("</div>")

```
**In this example**:

LOESS achieved a **lower test error** than BART when predicting acceleration from time in the mcycle dataset. This result is expected because the relationship in the data is smooth and one-dimensional, which aligns well with the assumptions behind LOESS. LOESS is specifically designed to capture local trends in such settings, making it highly effective here. In contrast, BART is a more `flexible, tree-based method` that can model complex and high-dimensional relationships, but that flexibility is not necessary for this dataset and can introduce additional variance. This comparison highlights an important idea: **more complex models do not always lead to better performance**, and choosing an appropriate method depends heavily on the structure of the data.
---



LOESS requires manual tuning of the span parameter, which directly controls the bias-variance tradeoff. Smaller spans reduce bias but increase variance, leading to overfitting, while larger spans increase bias. In contrast, BART performs automatic regularization through Bayesian averaging over many trees, resulting in more stable training and testing error without requiring manual tuning.
---

## Regression: Model Fit Comparison

```{r, eval=TRUE, echo=FALSE, results='asis', fig.width=4, fig.height=3,  out.width="40%"}
library(ggplot2)

# LOESS predictions on test data
loess_test_pred_best <- predict(
  loess_fit_best,
  newdata = data.frame(x_train = x_test)
)

# BART mean predictions on test data
bart_mean_pred <- colMeans(bart_test_draws)

# Data frames for plotting
loess_plot_df <- data.frame(
  Time = x_test,
  Acceleration = y_test,
  Prediction = loess_test_pred_best
)

bart_plot_df <- data.frame(
  Time = x_test,
  Acceleration = y_test,
  Prediction = bart_mean_pred
)

# LOESS plot
ggplot(loess_plot_df, aes(x = Time, y = Acceleration)) +
  geom_point(color = "gray") +
  geom_line(aes(y = Prediction), color = "blue", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "LOESS Fit (Test Data)",
    x = "Time",
    y = "Acceleration"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

# BART plot
ggplot(bart_plot_df, aes(x = Time, y = Acceleration)) +
  geom_point(color = "gray") +
  geom_line(aes(y = Prediction), color = "darkgreen", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "BART Fit (Test Data)",
    x = "Time",
    y = "Acceleration"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

```

**Lastly**:

Looking at the model fit plots, LOESS follows the smooth curve of the mcycle data more closely because it performs local smoothing. BART also captures the general pattern, but its prediction curve is less smooth because it is based on averaging many tree-based models, while still capturing the main trend in the data.
---
